content.problemStatement:
You are currently in charge of a large multinational company. You have many projects in hand. There are **N** employees currently in your company, all of whom have a unique ID, numbered from **1** to **N**. You want to form a non-empty set of teams of equal sizes from these employees such that each employee works in exactly one team. Your task is to find the number of ways to form such sets of teams.

Two ways are different if there exists at least one pair of employees who are in the same team in one way but belong to different teams in the other way.

There is an interesting property of **N**: it can be expressed as **p1×p2p_1 \times p_2p1​×p2​**, where both **p1p_1p1​** and **p2p_2p2​** are prime numbers (**p1p_1p1​** may be equal to **p2p_2p2​**) and **Max(p1,p2)<10×Min(p1,p2)\text{Max}(p_1, p_2) < 10 \times \text{Min}(p_1, p_2)Max(p1​,p2​)<10×Min(p1​,p2​)**. You need to output the number of ways modulo **q**, where **q** will always be a prime number.


Input Format
The first line of each input contains a single integer **T**, which denotes the number of test cases. Each case starts with a line containing two integers, **L** and **q**, where **L** is the number of lines to follow and **q** is a prime number.


Each of the next **L** lines contains a single integer **N**.



Output Format
For each test case, output "**Case :**" in a separate line, where **x** denotes the test case number. Then for each line containing **N**, print the number of ways modulo **q**. Follow Sample Input and Output for details.
